nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated headwaters and minor tributaries with rocky substrate; cobble, pebble, sand, and gravel beds covered in biofilm and algae; Pearl River system and adjacent drainages in southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan, Guizhou, Hunan, Jiangxi), Hong Kong, Hainan Island, and northeastern Vietnam

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn rocks and flat stones for grazing and resting
strong flow from powerhead or wavemaker (10–15× turnover per hour)
strong lighting to promote algae and diatom growth on hard surfaces

diet.notes: The Broken-Band Hillstream Loach is a constant grazer that scrapes biofilm, diatoms, algae (including cyanobacteria), and small microorganisms from hard surfaces — a feeding strategy shared by most hillstream loaches in the wild. In an aquarium, this natural grazing should be supplemented with algae wafers, spirulina tablets, blanched vegetables such as zucchini or cucumber, and sinking gel foods placed directly on grazing rocks. Occasional small live or frozen invertebrates such as bloodworm or Daphnia are accepted and help with conditioning. Adequate biofilm on tank surfaces is essential; fish are prone to starvation in low-flow or newly set-up tanks that have not yet developed established algae growth. A biologically mature tank with algae-coated rocks greatly improves long-term survival.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches sharing similar flow and temperature requirements
small cool-water cyprinids (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, Danios)
small peaceful loaches tolerant of high flow
small rasboras in cooler end of their range
Amano shrimp and larger ornamental shrimp in high-flow setups
avoidWith: boisterous or fin-nipping species that outcompete for food
slow-water species requiring low flow (gouramis, bettas, fancy guppies)
Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158) in small tanks — territorial competition likely between similar bottom-dwelling grazers

summary: A compact hillstream loach from southern China and Vietnam that clings to rocks in fast-moving rapids, grazing on biofilm, algae, and microorganisms. Demands cool water (20–24°C), very high flow, and high dissolved oxygen; frequently starves in low-flow setups. Distinguished from the Butterfly Hillstream Loach by its broken horizontal bar pattern.
careNotes:
The Broken-Band Hillstream Loach is a fast-water specialist native to shallow, highly oxygenated mountain streams across southern China (the Pearl River system and adjacent drainages), Vietnam, and Hong Kong. It is named for the broken horizontal bars along its flanks, which distinguish it from the more commonly traded Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158), which displays a striped and spotted dorsal pattern. This entry covers both the nominate population from southern China and Vietnam and the Hainan Island population, which is recognised as a subspecies of the same species.

Successful long-term care depends on replicating its natural rapids habitat. Filtration should achieve 10–15× tank volume turnover per hour; a dedicated powerhead or wavemaker aimed across flat grazing stones is strongly recommended. High dissolved oxygen is essential — surface agitation must be vigorous. Water temperature must be kept in the 20–24°C range; temperatures above 25°C increase disease susceptibility and reduce the oxygen concentration the fish depends on. A biologically mature tank with established algae, diatom, and biofilm colonies on rocks and driftwood is essential before introducing this species; fish routinely starve in newly set-up or sterile tanks. Strong lighting promotes the algal growth on hard surfaces that this species grazes constantly.

Supplement natural biofilm with algae wafers, spirulina tablets, and blanched vegetables placed directly on grazing rocks. Occasional small live or frozen invertebrates such as bloodworm or Daphnia are accepted and beneficial for conditioning.

Keep in groups of four or more; lone specimens or pairs tend to show less natural behaviour, and females are socially dominant within the group. A tight-fitting lid is essential — these loaches are capable climbers and escape readily. Unlike many hillstream loaches, this species does not defend a permanent personal territory around a single stone; groups range across the tank and benefit from ample horizontal surface area, which is why a minimum tank length of 80 cm is strongly advised.

Not suitable for standard warm-water community setups. Best kept in a species-appropriate hillstream biotope or alongside cool-water companions such as White Cloud Mountain Minnows or small danios that share the same flow and temperature requirements.
breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner; reproduction in the wild corresponds to the dry season in southern China and Hong Kong (approximately October–April). In captivity, conditioning with varied high-quality foods and a seasonal temperature drop may trigger spawning. Females are dominant and excavate shallow depressions in substrate or between stones as egg deposition sites. Eggs are approximately 1 mm in diameter, orangish in colour; they hatch in roughly 3 days at 22°C. Fry absorb yolk over a further 3–5 days before emerging at 6–8 mm total length. Sexual maturity is reached within one year. Breeding in the home aquarium is uncommon but has been reported in mature, well-maintained hillstream biotope setups.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (36–268 ppm), native range (Pearl River system, southern China provinces, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Hainan), habitat (fast-flowing oxygenated headwaters, rocky substrate), tank base dimensions (80×30 cm), filter turnover guidance (10–15×), diet (biofilm, algae, small invertebrates), group size (4+), breeding details (seasonal Oct–Apr, egg hatch ~3 days, fry 6–8 mm), and care level (advanced — downgraded to intermediate per project brief).

additional.name: FishBase — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 8.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–24°C), distribution (Asia: China and Vietnam), habitat (babbling bottom of mountain streams, demersal, freshwater), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), trophic level 2.7 (herbivore/detritivore). IUCN Least Concern (assessed 2024). No pH, hardness, or depth data available.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General hillstream loach guide (primarily Sewellia/reticulated types); confirmed temperature range 20–27°C for hillstream loaches broadly, supplemental feeding with sinking wafers and blanched vegetables, group housing of 3+, tight-fitting lid requirement, and compatibility with peaceful community fish of similar size. No L. disparis-specific data; used for general husbandry corroboration.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is uncommon but has been reported in mature, well-maintained hillstream biotope setups. This species is a seasonal spawner; in the wild, reproduction corresponds to the dry season in southern China and Hong Kong, roughly October through April. Females are socially dominant and control the selection of egg deposition sites. Eggs hatch in approximately three days, and fry emerge at 6–8 mm total length after absorbing their yolk. Sexual maturity is typically reached within one year.
